### Step 2: Log into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#step-2-log-into-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have connected, you may see a password prompt to log in.

![multi login - access](../_images/multi-login.png)

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

![multi login - TOTP already configured](../_images/multi-noop.png)

Otherwise, a QR code and secret key will be printed.

![multi login - QR generated](../_images/multi-qr.png)

*Please be careful not to point your camera at the example QR code displayed on the web page, as it will happily read it but this code is not valid for any purpose. You want to use* **your** *QR code from* **your** *login session to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.*

### Step 3: Scan the QR code or enter the secret key.[¶](#step-3-scan-the-qr-code-or-enter-the-secret-key "Permalink to this headline")

In your chosen authenticator app, you will want to create a new entry for your CSD3 SSH Login TOTP. In this walkthrough, we use Google Authenticator and Microsoft Authenticator as examples.

Both Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator display a ‘+’ icon for adding a new account:

> * **Google Authenticator** then presents a choice of *Scan a QR code* or *Enter a setup key*, i.e. either point your phone camera at the displayed QR'}, {'rank': 3, 'doc_id': 'https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html::chunk::0004', 'score': 0.015873015873015872, 'source': 'docs', 'retrieval_sources': ['docs'], 'retrieval_source_ranks': {'docs': 3}, 'retrieval_signal_trace': {'dense_rank': 3, 'dense_score': 0.7128018, 'signal_type': 'dense'}, 'source_authority': 'local_official', 'authority_tier': 3, 'validity_status': 'current', 'freshness_hint': None, 'title': None, 'doc_title': None, 'system_names': [], 'partition_names': [], 'service_names': ['MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)'], 'scope_family_names': [], 'software_names': [], 'software_versions': [], 'module_names': [], 'toolchain_names': [], 'toolchain_versions': [], 'official_doc_matches': [{'entity_id': '9597fbf64af1ee9a', 'entity_type': 'service', 'canonical_name': 'MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)', 'doc_id': 'https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html', 'doc_title': 'MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)', 'source_scope': 'local_official', 'status': 'current', 'match_methods': ['doc_id_join'], 'matched_aliases': [], 'matched_versions': []}], 'rerank_trace': {'reranker_type': 'rrf', 'rrf_k': 60, 'rrf_score': 0.015873015873015872, 'source_ranks': {'docs': 3}, 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0}}, 'text': 'code, or type in the “secret key”, respectively.
> * **Microsoft Authenticator** presents a choice of new account type, choose *Other account*. This will activate the camera - point it at the displayed QR code.

**Mac Users:** You may find that the QR code appears distorted or broken comapred to the example found on this page. In most cases it should still scan as normal, however, you may need to enter the secret key manually as described above if the QR code does not scan.

The correct TOTP created in this way will appear in your mobile app under the account **CSD3: SSH Login**. Please note that a TOTP created for use with login-web appears as **CSD3: your username**.

### Step 4: Confirm your TOTP setup[¶](#step-4-confirm-your-totp-setup "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have successfully got your CSD3 SSH Login TOTP on your chosen authenticator app, you may now check the TOTP generation works by selecting the new account and entering the 6-digit number at the prompt on your temrinal. Alternatively, skip this step by entering *-1*.

![multi login - TOTP code input](../_images/multi-confirmation.png)

### Step 5: Make a note of your one-time passcodes.[¶](#step-5-make-a-note-of-your-one-time-passcodes "Permalink to this headline")

Mandatory MultiFactor Authentication (MFA) was introduced to CSD3 and storage services logins on 1st November 2022 (you can read the archived [full maintenance here](/hpc/full_maintenance-Nov2022.html)).

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are having trouble setting up your MFA, please consult the [FAQs](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#frequently-asked-questions-faq) or watch our [video tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OvTgniwVxEg&t=365s)

## What does this mean?[¶](#what-does-this-mean "Permalink to this headline")

As well as your passwords and SSH keys, when you use the services, a *Time-based One-Time Password* (TOTP) code will be requested. This is a six digit number, refreshed every 30 seconds, which is generated typically by an app running on your mobile phone. Thus authentication will then require at least two “factors”, i.e. either password or SSH key (the first factor), plus TOTP (the second factor).

Two common such mobile apps (available from mobile app stores and tested by us) are *Google Authenticator* and *Microsoft Authenticator*, which indeed you may already have and be using for other services.

### Step 0: Watch our video guide[¶](#step-0-watch-our-video-guide "Permalink to this headline")

If you are new to SSH and terminal use, we highly recommend that you follow the [video tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OvTgniwVxEg&t=365s)

### Step 1: Connect to special MFA node[¶](#step-1-connect-to-special-mfa-node "Permalink to this headline")

When you are ready to configure TOTP, log into the special node *multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk*, via a terminal (not a browser). The sole function of this node is to set up TOTP if you currently have none configured.

To access the special MFA node:

```
ssh <username>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

The first time you connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (as for any new destination with SSH) you will see a message similar to this:

```
The authenticity of host 'multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (128.232.222.63)' can't be established.
ED25519 key fingerprint is SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so.
```

Please check that the fingerprint listed matches one of those listed as current at <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html>.

You may also see additional lines like the following:

```
This host key is known by the following other names/addresses:
    ~/.ssh/known_hosts:18: login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
    (4 additional names omitted)
```

This is to be expected as all login nodes on CSD3 share a single set of host keys. Provided the key fingerprint is current it is ok to respond **yes** to the final question:

## How do I reset my TOTP for SSH to CSD3?[¶](#how-do-i-reset-my-totp-for-ssh-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

If you need to reset your TOTP second factor, e.g. because you no longer have access to your original phone, please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk.
The TOTP second factor is an important protection against user account compromise, and an identity check is therefore needed when a request to replace it is received. This is similar to the existing process for resetting University passwords in a context where face to face identity checks are not feasible most of the time.

When contacting the service desk to request a reset of MFA, please be sure to send the email from the email address we have registered against your user account.

The service desk agent will arrange a time at which to hold a brief video call with you at which you will be asked to present photographic proof of identity in the form of one of the following:

> * Passport
> * Driving licence
> * University card (for members of the University of Cambridge).

Expired forms of the above are acceptable as proof of identity. user_input:
Hello,
I hope this email finds you well!
I am working on CSD3 for some cpu jobs, particularly nodes cclake and Icelake. I submitted some jobs last week and I found the queueing time very long. I am wondering if you can give advice on this regarding if there is anything I did wrong causing the long wait. Some general info:
- Name: Hongyu Zhou
- Account/PI: N-LAWRENCE-SL3-CPU
- Working directory: /home/hz488/project/ai_ranking_report
- Task:
- Data: I work with bibliographic data on hundreds of millions of paper including their title, abstract, and citation relationships
- Task:
- I am trying to calculate the cooccurence frequency of concepts in papers (two concepts used in one paper), for which the concepts are extracted already in our current data (one paper associated with a list of paper).
- I am building these cooccurrence networks for using papers since 1990 to current and group them to a interval of 3 years
- I generate networks for every three years.
- Problem:
- The queueing time is quite long.
- Slurm Job_id=10784619 Name=build_cooccur_net Began, Queued time 17:19:49
- I tried to minimise the potential RAM usage by using smaller intervals, using smaller samples and use sql-like ducked to calculate the concurrences. But in the future, there will definitely be larger jobs I need to run considering the size of my data in nature, especially if we were use Node2Vec to embed the networks we obtained.
- Job submission script:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J build_cooccur_net
#SBATCH -A N-LAWRENCE-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=6
#SBATCH --mem=30G
#SBATCH --time=4:00:00
#SBATCH --output=1_build_net_duck_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=1_build_net_duck_%j.err
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=hz488@cam.ac.uk
set -euo pipefail
echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %T')] Job started"
module purge
module load python/3.8
source "$HOME/hzenv/bin/activate"
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=1
export OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS=1
TEMP_DIR="${SLURM_TMPDIR:-./temp_edges}"
mkdir -p "$TEMP_DIR"
python /home/hz488/project/ai_ranking_report/scripts/1_build_net_save_duckdb.py \
--input-dir /home/hz488/project/dimensions/publications/ \
--output-dir /home/hz488/project/data/ai_ranking_report/data/1_build_net \
--batch-size 200000 \
--batches-per-file 50 \
--sample-frac 1
echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %T')] Job finished”
Could you kindly advise me on this?
Best,
Hongyu

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Submission / Queue Time
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing long queueing times for their jobs on the CSD3 cluster, specifically on the icelake and cclake nodes [1][2]. The user has provided their job submission script, which includes directives for the SLURM batch system [2][3]. The script requests 6 CPUs per task, 30G of memory, and a wall time of 4 hours [2]. The user has tried to minimize RAM usage by using smaller intervals and samples, but still experiences long queueing times [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To address the long queueing times, we recommend checking the job's status and queue position using the `squeue` command [3]. Additionally, we suggest reviewing the job submission script to ensure that the requested resources are available on the cluster [2][3]. If the job requires more than 3.4GB per CPU, it may be automatically assigned extra CPUs, increasing the charge [5]. We also recommend considering the use of the `-long` partitions, such as `icelake-long` or `cclake-long`, which allow for longer wall times [1][7].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more information about the job's dependencies and requirements?
- Have you tried submitting the job to a different partition, such as `icelake-himem` or `cclake-himem`?
- Are there any specific error messages or warnings in the job's output or error files?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your email. We recommend checking the job's status and queue position using the `squeue` command. Additionally, please review your job submission script to ensure that the requested resources are available on the cluster. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to contact us.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be cautious when requesting more resources than necessary, as this can increase the charge [5].
- Always review the job submission script to ensure that the requested resources are available on the cluster [2][3].
- Consider using the `-long` partitions for jobs that require longer wall times [1][7].

CSD3[¶](#running-jobs-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

SLURM is an open source workload management and job scheduling system. Research Computing clusters adopted SLURM in February 2014, but previously used Torque, Maui/Moab and Gold (referred to in the following simply as “PBS”) for the same purpose. Please note that there are several commands available in SLURM with the same names as in PBS (e.g. showq, qstat, qsub, qdel, qrerun) intended for backwards compatibility, but in general we would recommend using the native SLURM commands, as described below.

If you have any questions on how to run jobs on CSD3 do not hesitate
to contact the support desk.

## Accounting Commands[¶](#accounting-commands "Permalink to this headline")

The following commands are wrappers around the underlying SLURM commands sacct and sreport which are much more powerful.

Note that project names in SLURM are not case sensitive.

**What resources do I have available to me?**

This is the first question to settle before submitting jobs to CSD3. Use the command

```
mybalance
```

to show your projects, your current usages and the remaining balances in compute unit hours.

On CSD3 we are using natural compute units for each component of the facility:

* on CPU nodes we are allocating and reporting in **CPU core hours**
* on GPU nodes we are allocating and reporting in **GPU hours**.

We have adopted the convention that projects containing CPU hours for use on CPU nodes will end in -CPU, while those holding GPU hours for use on GPU nodes end in -GPU.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```

#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

New user accounts also have symbolic links to template files in their home directories. Lines beginning #SBATCH are directives to the batch system. #SBATCH -A MYPROJECT-CPU
#! Which partition/cluster am I using?
#SBATCH -p cclake
#! How many nodes should be allocated? If not specified SLURM
#! assumes 1 node.
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many tasks will there be in total? By default SLURM
#! will assume 1 task per node and 1 CPU per task.
#SBATCH --ntasks=56
#! How much memory in MB is required _per node_? Not setting this
#! as here will lead to a default amount per task.
#! Setting a larger amount per task increases the number of CPUs.
##SBATCH --mem=
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=02:00:00
```

in particular, the name of the project is required for the job to be scheduled (use the command mybalance to check what this is for you in case of doubt). Charging is reported in units of compute hours (what these represent depends on the cluster).

This page describes how suitable applications requiring unusually long execution times can be run on CSD3.

## Definition of long jobs[¶](#definition-of-long-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

We define long jobs as jobs requiring wall times (i.e. real execution times) of up to **7 days**. Continuous execution times of these lengths are normally disallowed by both non-paying and paying qualities of service (QoS), in order to achieve reasonable overall throughput.

In general it is advisable for any application running for extended periods to have the ability to save its progress (i.e. to checkpoint) as insurance against unexpected failures that may result in wastage of significant resources. Applications for which it is possible to checkpoint are largely immune from per-job runtime limits as they can simply resume from the most recent checkpoint in the guise of a new job. Applications for which it is not feasible to checkpoint may find the scheduling features described below to be of use.

Access to the long job QoS is not given by default; if you have a need to submit such jobs please contact support describing your case.

## The QOSL QoS[¶](#the-qosl-qos "Permalink to this headline")

Paying users with suitable applications may be granted access to the QOSL quality of service, which permits jobs running for up to 7 days. Jobs associated with this special QoS are confined to `-long` variants of the usual partitions (`icelake-long`, `cclake-long` and `ampere-long`). user_input:
Dear HPC team,
Good afternoon, I am contacting you since I am trying to run Freesurfer 8.0.0 on HPC but I am getting the following error:
FREESURFER: Undefined variable.
This makes me wonder if for some reason the path to freesurfer cannot be found. Since this is a new module can you please advise on the path where the application is so that I can use in my scripts? Has this been tested and was running as intended?
Needless to say I have loaded the module.
Best wishes,
Marialena
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Module Integration / Environment Setup
Technical Action Required: Yes (module/setup correction)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User loaded `freesurfer/8.0.0` and received `FREESURFER: Undefined variable`. From the original query, the most likely issue is incomplete environment setup after loading the module rather than the software being absent. The next step is to reproduce the module load, check the exact command or script the user is running, and then handle any further setup requirements such as the FreeSurfer setup script or licence configuration if they arise.

ACTION
1) Reproduce the issue and validate module behaviour.
2) Ask the user to show the exact commands or job script they are using.
3) Instruct the standard setup sequence:
   - `module load freesurfer/8.0.0`
   - `source $FREESURFER_HOME/SetUpFreeSurfer.sh`
4) If a subsequent error indicates a missing licence file, instruct the user to obtain a valid licence and set `FS_LICENSE=/path/to/license.txt`.
5) Confirm successful execution after the environment is set correctly.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Marialena,

Please first try the standard setup sequence:

module load freesurfer/8.0.0
source $FREESURFER_HOME/SetUpFreeSurfer.sh

Then retry the command that is failing.

If you then receive a separate licence-file error, please send that exact message and we can advise on setting `FS_LICENSE` correctly.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not request users place the licence file in protected system directories.
- Do not assume the initial undefined-variable error proves the software is missing or broken.

Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing an issue with running Freesurfer 8.0.0 on HPC, receiving an error message indicating an undefined variable [1]. This suggests a potential issue with the environment configuration or module loading [2][3]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, the user should first verify that the Freesurfer module is loaded correctly. The user can check the available modules using the `module avail` command [3] and load the desired module using `module load <module_name>` [6]. If the issue persists, the user should provide more details about their environment configuration, such as the output of `module list` [5] and any modifications made to their `.bashrc` file [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact error message and the command used to run Freesurfer?
- Have you made any changes to your `.bashrc` file or environment configuration recently?
- Can you provide the output of `module list` and `module avail` commands?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To troubleshoot the issue with running Freesurfer 8.0.0, please verify that the correct module is loaded and provide the output of `module list` and `module avail` commands. If you have made any changes to your environment configuration, please let us know. We will be happy to assist you further.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always verify the environment configuration and module loading before running software on HPC [1][2][3].
- Be cautious when modifying the `.bashrc` file, as it can affect the default environment settings [4].

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

The default environment should be correctly established automatically via the modules system and the shell initialization scripts. For example, essential system software for compilation, credit and quota management, job execution and scheduling, error-correcting wrappers and MPI recommended settings are all applied in this way. This works by setting the `PATH` and `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` environment variables, amongst others, to particular values. Please be careful when editing your `~/.bashrc` file, if you wish to do so, as this can wreck the default settings and create many problems if done incorrectly, potentially rendering the account unusable until administrative intervention. In particular, if you wish to modify `PATH` or `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` please be sure to preserve the existing settings, e.g. do

```
export PATH=/your/custom/path/element:$PATH
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/your/custom/librarypath/element:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
```

and don’t simply overwrite the existing values, or you will have problems. If you are trying to add directories relating to centrally-installed software, please note that there is probably a module available which can be loaded to adjust the environment correctly.

Users who are returning to CSD3 after a few years should check their `~/.bashrc` files and if necessary DELETE any pre-existing lines such as these:

```
module load default-impi
module load default-wilkes
module load rhel7/default-peta4
```

since these will now interfere with the proper environment settings on CSD3 nodes.

Further commands:

```
module load <module>         -> load module
module unload <module>       -> unload module
module purge                 -> unload all modules
module list                  -> show currently loaded modules
module avail                 -> show available modules
module whatis                -> show available modules with brief explanation
```

There may be a number of historical modules inherited from older systems that appear in response to `module avail`. Please avoid modules with names such as “sandybridge” and “nehalem” in their paths and prefer modules exhibiting the Spack hash (-4qrgkot in the example above).

### Loading, unloading and swapping modules[¶](#loading-unloading-and-swapping-modules "Permalink to this headline")

To load a module to use `module add` or `module load`. For example,
to load the pgi commpiler into the development environment:

```
module load pgi
```

This will load the default version of the pgi commpilers Library. If
you need a specfic version of the module, you can add more information:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will remove a loaded module:

```
module remove pgi
```

(or `module rm pgi` or
`module unload pgi`) will unload whatever version of pgi (even if it is not the default) you might have
loaded.

## Private and group modules[¶](#private-and-group-modules "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to create private modules using the following procedure:

1. Create a folder named `privatemodules` in the home directory with:

```
cd ~
mkdir privatemodules
```

2. Add module files in the `privatemodules` folder
3. Access the private modules with:

```
module load use.own
```

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login Node Health / Temporary Filesystem
Technical Action Required: Yes (diagnose node-specific issue)
Escalation Required: Optional (if persistent)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports compiler warning that `/tmp` is full and asks whether this relates to their own quota. `/tmp` is node-local temporary storage and can fill independently of the user’s `/home` or project quota.

ACTION
1) Explain what `/tmp` is:
   - temporary local filesystem on the node,
   - not the same as user/project quota shown by `quota`.
2) Ask user to report the node where error occurs (`hostname`).
3) Suggest immediate workaround: try compiling on another login node.
4) Escalate if issue persists across multiple nodes or for multiple users.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Loren,

The warning about `/tmp` means temporary space on the current node is full. This is separate from your `/home`/project quota, so your `quota` output can still look normal.

Please share the output of:
`hostname`

As a workaround, please try compiling on a different login node. If the issue appears on multiple nodes, let us know and we will escalate.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume user quota issue when warning explicitly references `/tmp`.

## Compiling[¶](#compiling "Permalink to this headline")

Note that the `default-X` module, which is loaded by default on nodes of type X, arranges for `mpicc`, `mpif90` etc to be found and to use the recommended compilers and MPI implementation automatically when invoked. These wrapper commands supply the correct flags for compiling with the particular MPI implementation to which they belong.

When compiling code, it is usually possible to remove any direct MPI library references in your Makefile as mpicc & mpif90 will take care of these details. In the Makefile, simply set

```
CC=mpicc
```

etc, or define

```
export CC=mpicc
```

etc in the shell before compilation.

If some required libraries are missing, please let us know and we can try to install them centrally (as a module).

## Running[¶](#running "Permalink to this headline")

Please note that the following resource limits apply:

On CPU, SL1 and SL2 users are limited to 4256 cores in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job. On GPU, SL1 and SL2 are limited to 64 GPUs in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job.
SL3 users are similarly limited to 448 cores (CPU) and 32 GPUs (GPU), all with up to 12 hours per job.
For more information, please see this full description of [service levels (SLs)](/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels).

Please see the example job submission scripts under `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM`. There are example scripts for launching an MPI application on CPU or GPU via'}, {'rank': 2, 'doc_id': 'https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html::chunk::0001', 'score': 0.016129032258064516, 'source': 'docs', 'retrieval_sources': ['docs'], 'retrieval_source_ranks': {'docs': 2}, 'retrieval_signal_trace': {'dense_rank': 2, 'dense_score': 0.47189766, 'signal_type': 'dense'}, 'source_authority': 'local_official', 'authority_tier': 3, 'validity_status': 'unknown', 'freshness_hint': None, 'title': None, 'doc_title': None, 'system_names': [], 'partition_names': [], 'service_names': [], 'scope_family_names': [], 'software_names': [], 'software_versions': [], 'module_names': [], 'toolchain_names': [], 'toolchain_versions': [], 'official_doc_matches': [], 'rerank_trace': {'reranker_type': 'rrf', 'rrf_k': 60, 'rrf_score': 0.016129032258064516, 'source_ranks': {'docs': 2}, 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0}}, 'text': 'latter are performant and scalable, and *it is on these that active data to be read or written by jobs should be placed* (not under /home which is more suited to code, post processed results, reports etc).

The hpc-work directories provide free, 1TB storage for the personal use of Cambridge researchers. This storage cannot be shared with other users - please purchase shared storage from the [Research Data Store (RDS)](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store). The hpc-work directories themselves are also provided by the RDS service. National Facility (DiRAC/Tier2/IRIS) users may already have additional project areas visible under /rds/user/<username> and ~/rds provided from pre-paid RDS storage which are shared between members of the same project.

Please note that for general users we currently provide two tiers only of non-transient storage (i.e. storage not limited to the runtime of a single job), namely */home* and *hpc-work* (the latter possibly supplemented by additional RDS storage areas if purchased). As stated above RDS directories are based on Lustre and are the correct targets for job I/O. The term *scratch* space if encountered should be understood to refer to these Lustre storage areas.

## Disk quotas[¶](#disk-quotas "Permalink to this headline")

## Summary of available filesystems[¶](#summary-of-available-filesystems "Permalink to this headline")

| Directory | Explanation | Backed up? | Disk quotas | Files removed? |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| /home/<username>/ | User home directory\*\* | Yes (hourly snapshots) | Yes (50GB) | No |
| /rds/user/<username>/hpc-work\* | HPC work user directories\*\* | No | Yes (1TB and 1 million files) | No automatic deletion currently |
| /local/ | Per-node local disk\*\*\* | No | Limited by partition size (57GB or 131GB) | Deleted after job completion |
| /ramdisks/ | Per-node tmpfs (in-memory)\*\*\* | No | Limited to 50% node RAM | Deleted after job completion |
| /usr/local/ | Cluster-wide software | Yes | N/A (not for user storage) | N/A (not for user storage) |

\*For convenience, directories under /rds/user/<username>/ can be reached by symbolic links (shortcuts) of the same name under ~/rds.

\*\*These are shared filesystems - files created on these are immediately visible to all nodes.

\*\*\*These are private to each compute node - files created on these are destroyed after completion of the current job.

### GCC Compiler Suite[¶](#gcc-compiler-suite "Permalink to this headline")

The GCC compiler suite is accessed by loading the `gcc` module. For example:

```
module load gcc-7.2.0-gcc-4.8.5-pqn7o2k

* `g++` - C++

### PGI Compiler Suite[¶](#pgi-compiler-suite "Permalink to this headline")

The Portland Group (PGI) compilers are available under the `pgi` modules. For example:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

Once you have loaded the module, the compilers are available as:

* `pgfortran` - Fortran
* `pgcc` - C
* `pg++` - C++

Dear Sir/Madam,
My name is Guillaume Alexis, id : grfa2. My working directory is /rds/user/grfa2/hpc-work/
For now, I don't have a job submission script. I'm solely trying to install a package named Dedalus ([https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/installation.html](https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/installation.html)) and its dependancies, in order to run my script.
My main question is : How should I do it ? I tried in a conda environment, but I'm unsure about the intel/intel-mpi/fftw/hdf5 versions I should load, in order to build Dedalus correctly with these modules.
Thank you for your help,
Best wishes,
Guillaume Alexis
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Python Environment Management / Package Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to install Dedalus while relying on the cluster-wide conda module. The supported path is to install a personal Miniconda/Miniforge instance in `hpc-work`, then follow the upstream installation method from that personal environment. The user does not need to manually choose cluster MPI/FFTW module versions if they install the package through the documented conda-forge workflow.

ACTION
1) Do not use the shared cluster conda module for this workflow.
2) Install Miniconda or Miniforge in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Create the environment there and follow the upstream Dedalus installation instructions.
4) Once installed, use Slurm or an interactive job as normal to run the code.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Guillaume,

Please avoid using the cluster’s shared conda module for this install. Instead, install your own Miniconda or Miniforge under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create the Dedalus environment there.

If you follow the Dedalus installation guidance from that personal environment, you should not need to manually select Intel MPI/FFTW module versions for the basic conda-forge install path.

Once the environment is ready, you can run the code via Slurm as usual, or use an interactive job if you want to test it first.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Prefer user-managed conda installs in `hpc-work` over the outdated shared conda module.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Installation / Environment Setup
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is attempting to install the Dedalus package and its dependencies on the HPC system [1]. They have tried using a conda environment but are unsure about the correct versions of intel, intel-mpi, fftw, and hdf5 to load [1]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To install Dedalus, we recommend using a miniconda environment, similar to the approach described for OpenMM [8]. First, load the necessary modules, including rhel8/default-amp [2]. Then, create a new conda environment and activate it. The user should then install the required dependencies, including intel, intel-mpi, fftw, and hdf5, using conda install [3]. After installing the dependencies, the user can attempt to build Dedalus.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the exact error message encountered when trying to build Dedalus?
- Have you tried loading the intel-mpi module, specifically Intel MPI 2021.6.0, as recommended for Sapphire Rapid nodes [1]?
- Can you provide more details about your conda environment and the packages you have installed so far?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Guillaume,

To install Dedalus, we recommend creating a new conda environment and installing the required dependencies, including intel, intel-mpi, fftw, and hdf5. Please try loading the intel-mpi module, specifically Intel MPI 2021.6.0, as recommended for Sapphire Rapid nodes. If you encounter any issues, please provide the exact error message and details about your conda environment.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When installing software packages, it is essential to follow the recommended installation procedures and load the correct modules to ensure compatibility with the HPC system [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/sapphire.html::chunk::0001
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/openmm.html::chunk::0002
[3] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/openmm.html::chunk::0001
[8] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/openmm.html::chunk::0000

'text': 'Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.peta4-icelake
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs using icelake, but can be modified for
other types of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts,
please see the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs not using MPI[¶](#jobs-not-using-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

In this case you should be able to simply specify the sapphire partition to
the -p sbatch directive, e.g.:

```
#SBATCH -p sapphire
```

will submit a job able to run on the first nodes available in the
sapphire partition.
If you need more than 4580 MiB of memory, you will need to specify it as a sbatch directive like this:

```
#SBATCH --mem=8000
```

to ask for 8000 MiB memory.

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *Intel MPI 2021.6.0* on Sapphire Rapid nodes. There are
other, related changes to the default environment seen by jobs running
on the sapphire nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against the
Sapphire Rapid environment, the simplest option is to use a login node as shown above. Alternatively, request an

## Running OpenMM on CSD3[¶](#running-openmm-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

To submit an OpenMM job please use the following submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#! Which project should be charged (NB Wilkes2 projects end in '-GPU'):
#SBATCH -A CHANGE_TO_YOUR_ACCOUNT-GPU
#! Change to relevant GPU cluster
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH -o openmm.out
#SBATCH -e openmm.err
#! How many whole nodes should be allocated?
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=00:10:00
#! What types of email messages do you wish to receive?
#SBATCH --mail-type=FAIL
#! Uncomment this to prevent the job from being requeued (e.g. if
#! interrupted by node failure or system downtime):
##SBATCH --no-requeue

#! sbatch directives end here (put any additional directives above this line)

#! Notes:
#! Charging is determined by GPU number*walltime.

#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
numtasks=$SLURM_NTASKS
mpi_tasks_per_node=$(echo "$SLURM_TASKS_PER_NODE" | sed -e  's/^\([0-9][0-9]*\).*$/\1/')

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. From the OpenFOAM website <http://www.openfoam.org>

> OpenFOAM(Field Operation And Manipulation) is free, open source software for CFD from the OpenFOAM Foundation.

## Running OpenFOAM on CSD3[¶](#running-openfoam-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

OpenFOAM contains a number of applications and utilities. Version 2312 is currently installed with Intel MPI and the GNU compilers.

Running OpenFOAM will require a simple script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A MYACCOUNT
#SBATCH -p cclake
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH -n 5 # Matching requirements from example below. Tune according to your own needs.
#SBATCH -t 02:00:00
#SBATCH -o openfoam.out
#SBATCH -e openfoam.err

module purge
module load rhel8/cclake/base # Use on either cclake, icelake or sapphire, as it is designed to work for all RHEL8 CPU partitions.
module load openfoam/2312/gcc/intel-oneapi-mpi/2ioxyvgw

# This is required to setup the proper variables and library paths
. $WM_PROJECT_DIR/bin/tools/RunFunctions
# Using a parallel example from the tutorials. Replace the following by your own project
# This example uses up to 5 parallel streams, hence -n 5 in the SBATCH header.
cp -R $WM_PROJECT_DIR/tutorials/mesh/parallel/cavity .
cd cavity
./Allclean
./Allrun
```

which can be submitted to the queue with `sbatch` from the same directory as the case directory. This will run on one node in pure MPI mode up to a limit of 2 hours. To run on more nodes the `-N` and `-n` options should be adjusted and the timelimit can be changed with `-t`.'}, {'rank': 8, 'doc_id': ## Installing OpenMM on CSD3[¶](#installing-openmm-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

Connect to a login node with:

```
ssh your-username@login.hpc.cam.ac.uk

mkdir -p miniconda3

bash miniconda3/miniconda.sh -b -u -p miniconda3

fi

user_input:
Good afternoon,
A student from CEB would like to use abaqus 2024 on the HPC cluster. The student mentioned that they contacted you and you advised that this version is not compatible.
I wanted to confirm if this is the case or did the end user misunderstand.
Kind regards,
Zac
------------------------------------------------------
Zac Iskander (he/him)
IT Support Team Leader
University of Cambridge
Department of Engineering
Trumpington Street
Cambridge, CB2 1PZ, UK
01223760591
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Version Support / Licensing
Technical Action Required: No (policy clarification)
Escalation Required: Yes (new build request if required)
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The immediate answer is that `abaqus/2024` is not currently available as a centrally supported version on the cluster; the latest supported central version is `abaqus/2022`. If there is a requirement for a newer version, licensing and a separate build or install request would need to be coordinated.

ACTION
1) Confirm that `abaqus/2022` is the latest supported central version.
2) Explain that no timeline can be promised for `abaqus/2024`.
3) If the relevant licence owner wants a newer centrally available version, ask them to submit a fresh build or install request to HPC support.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Zac,

At present the newest centrally supported version on the cluster is `abaqus/2022`, so the user did not misunderstand.

If the relevant licence owner wants a newer centrally available version, please send a new request to `support@hpc.cam.ac.uk` and we can look into building and providing that version.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:

Escalation Required: Possibly

There is no direct evidence in the retrieved documents that Abaqus 2024 is incompatible with the HPC cluster [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]. However, the documents do provide information on the available software and hardware on the cluster, as well as guidelines for running jobs and accessing the cluster [4][5][6][7][8].

To confirm whether Abaqus 2024 is compatible with the HPC cluster, the user should submit a ticket to the support team with detailed information about their issue, including any error messages they have received [1][2]. The support team can then investigate and provide guidance on how to proceed.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)

- Has the user tried running Abaqus 2024 on a different node or partition on the cluster [5][6]?

Best regards,
HPC Support

- When troubleshooting issues with software compatibility, it is essential to provide detailed information about the issue, including any error messages received [1][2].

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

InfiniBand. The GPU sub-cluster is also known as Wilkes3-GPU in its
own right. The name “Peta4” was associated historically with the
original CPU elements of CSD3.

'text': '# Sapphire Rapid Nodes[¶](#sapphire-rapid-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

*These new nodes entered general service in July 2023.*

## Slurm partitions[¶](#slurm-partitions "Permalink to this headline")

* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-r-[1-112]*.
* Some of the Sapphire Rapid nodes are in the **sapphire** Slurm partition. Your existing
  -CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to these.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes have **112 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and **4580 MiB RAM per cpu** for a total of 512 GB RAM per node.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are running [Rocky Linux 8](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r nodes are running Rocky 8, you may need to recompile
your applications.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-q-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 4:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p sapphire
```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partition[¶](#slurm-partition "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC (pvc-s) nodes are in a new **pvc9** Slurm partition.

Dawn Slurm projects follow the CSD3 naming convention for GPU projects and contain
units of GPU hours. * If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Login-Web is a “one stop shop” for access to our High Performance Computing resources. With Login-Web, it is possible to upload and download files, create, edit, submit, run GUI applications, and connect to nodes via SSH, all via a web browser, with no client software to install and configure.

## System Requirements[¶](#system-requirements "Permalink to this headline")

Currently, the site is confirmed to work with newer versions of Chrome (22+), Firefox (16+), and Microsoft Edge (11+). We are still working to expand availability to additional clients, and are planning on including compatibility for mobile devices (phones and tablets) in the future.

Note

When logging in for the first time to Login-Web, you will be required to set up MFA, please see the MFA documentation for more information: <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html>

If you need to reconfigure your MFA please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk and request a token reset.

### Connecting[¶](#connecting "Permalink to this headline")

To connect to Login-Web, visit <https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk>. The first page of the Login-Web site redirects you to the login page.

Tip

For a video guide on how to use Login-Web for CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

### Logging in[¶](#logging-in "Permalink to this headline")

user_input:
Hi Team,
I’m getting in touch regarding an issue on the SRCP platform, which I’ve attached for reference. We urgently need the SNPolisher R package installed into the environment associated with the following platform ID: kjtcs2jvzw7.
This is time‑critical, as the NHSBT Genomics Programme is currently unable to carry out essential clinical work without this package being available.
Could you please prioritise this installation at your earliest opportunity and confirm once it has been completed?
Grateful
[cid:b9547aa4-5bed-4828-8cca-804065ce11a4]
Many thanks,
Justin Ofosu-Dankwa, PhD
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Package Deployment / R Module
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: High

QUICK ASSESSMENT
This is an urgent request to make the SNPolisher package available on a specific SRCP platform. The correct initial support path is to prioritise escalation to the SRCP/platform team for package installation. Once availability is confirmed, the next step is to validate the package in a plain R session before troubleshooting any surrounding workflow/tool integration.

ACTION
1) Escalate the request to the SRCP/platform team as a priority package-installation request.
2) Once the package is reported available, validate it in a plain R session.
3) If the user's external tool still fails after installation, identify the tool name and R path/environment it uses.
4) Update the user once installation status has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Justin,

We are treating this as a priority request and are arranging installation of SNPolisher with the SRCP/platform team.

Once the package is available, we will send you the relevant load instructions and, if needed, follow up with checks to make sure your workflow is using the correct R environment.

Best regards,
HPC Support

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user requires the installation of the SNPolisher R package into a specific environment associated with the platform ID kjtcs2jvzw7, which is time-critical for the NHSBT Genomics Programme [1]. The retrieved documents provide guidance on installing R packages [1] and managing environments on CSD3 [2][3][4][5][6][7][8], but do not directly address the installation of SNPolisher.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To install the SNPolisher R package, follow these steps:
1. Load the appropriate R module, for example, `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` or `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph`, depending on the partition and requirements [1].
2. Start an interactive R session using `R` [1].
3. Install the SNPolisher package using `install.packages("SNPolisher")` [1].
4. Verify the installation by loading the package and checking its version.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the exact error message or issue encountered when trying to install or use SNPolisher?
- Are there specific dependencies or versions required for SNPolisher that are not met in the current environment?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To install the SNPolisher R package, please follow the steps outlined above. If you encounter any issues, please provide the exact error message and we will assist further. Please note that installing packages in your own environment is recommended, as centrally installed packages may not be up-to-date or compatible with all requirements [1].

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always verify the package name and version to ensure compatibility and correctness [1].
- Be cautious when installing packages to avoid version conflicts or dependencies issues [1][2].

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. ### Installing additional Anaconda Python modules[¶](#installing-additional-anaconda-python-modules "Permalink to this headline")

If the central `base` installation does not have a package or module that you require, you
can install this yourself by using *conda environments*.

A conda environemnt is a local copy of the central install that you can then modify
with additional modules/packages or even use different versions of existing
packages.

Full documentation on using conda environments can be found online at [Managing
conda environments](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html).

*biopython* package.

## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partitions[¶](#slurm-partitions "Permalink to this headline")

* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-r-[1-112]*.
* Some of the Sapphire Rapid nodes are in the **sapphire** Slurm partition. Your existing
  -CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to these.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes have **112 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and **4580 MiB RAM per cpu** for a total of 512 GB RAM per node.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are running [Rocky Linux 8](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r nodes are running Rocky 8, you may need to recompile
your applications.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-q-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 4:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p sapphire
```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

user_input:
Hi
I've been getting an error on Ampere jobs recently where it fails immediately, stating that the CUDA-capable devices aren't available. I've had this error occur when using submission scripts for running turbostream 3.6.10 which has worked for years, and using a separate script running turbostream 4.2.127 recently. I've attached an example slurm.out file I've had from a turbostream4 job. This error seems to happen whenever I get assigned gpu-q-29, though I've heard colleagues mention getting something similar recently on different nodes. I'm aware I can exclude gpu-q-29 in my sbatch, but if it happens on other nodes and I lose all the time I waited in the queue, it's frustrating. Are there any sbatch instructions I can add to avoid this happening, or a fix for the route problem?
Many Thanks,
Dom

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU Runtime Failure / Node Health / Slurm Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The job is intermittently failing on a specific GPU node with CUDA device availability errors. Two grounded mitigations from the submitted command/output are to ensure the correct Ampere base module stack is loaded and to let Open MPI infer task placement from Slurm instead of forcing `-npernode` and `-np`.

ACTION
1) Load the correct base stack:
   `module purge`
   `module load rhel8/default-amp`
2) Remove manual `mpirun -npernode ... -np ...` overrides where possible.
3) Prefer `--tasks-per-node=4` rather than only `--ntasks=8` for the reported two-node case.
4) As a temporary workaround, route around the bad node with:
   `#SBATCH --exclude=gpu-q-29`
5) If the issue persists after these changes or starts affecting other nodes, escalate for node-health investigation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Dom,

Please first make sure your script loads `rhel8/default-amp`, as that adds the expected CUDA runtime environment.

We also recommend removing the explicit `-npernode`/`-np` arguments and letting Open MPI infer task placement from Slurm. For a two-node, eight-task job, `--tasks-per-node=4` is the safer Slurm-side request.

In the short term, you can avoid the problematic node with:
`#SBATCH --exclude=gpu-q-29`

Best wishes,
HPC Support

The per-job, per-user GPU limits are currently 64 and 32 GPUs for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for ampere[¶](#default-submission-script-for-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

You should find a symbolic link to an Ampere default job submission script modified for the ampere nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.wilkes3
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs, but can be modified for other types
of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts, please see
the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs requiring N GPUs where N < 4[¶](#jobs-requiring-n-gpus-where-n-4 "Permalink to this headline")

Although there are 4 GPUs in each node it is possible to request fewer than this, e.g. to request 3 GPUs use:

```
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
#SBATCH -p ampere
```

Slurm will enforce allocation of a proportional number of CPUs (32) per GPU.

### Submitting the job to the queuing system[¶](#submitting-the-job-to-the-queuing-system "Permalink to this headline")

The command sbatch is used to submit jobs, e.g.

```
sbatch submission_script
```

The command will return a unique job identifier, which is used to query and control the job and to identify output. See the man page (*man sbatch*) for more options.

The following more complex example submits a job array with index values between 1 and 7 with a step size of 2 (i.e. 1, 3, 5 and 7) to the project STARS-SL2-CPU:

```
sbatch --array=1-7:2 -A STARS-SL2-CPU submission_script
```

### Deleting jobs[¶](#deleting-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

To cancel a job (either running or still queuing) use scancel:

```
scancel <jobid>
```

The <jobid> is printed when the job is submitted, alternatively use the commands squeue, qstat or showq to obtain the job ID.

The Ampere (A100) nodes are:

### Jobs requiring multiple nodes[¶](#jobs-requiring-multiple-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

Multi-node jobs need to request either exclusive access to the nodes, or 4 GPUs per node, i.e.:

```
#SBATCH --exclusive
```

or

```
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *the version of OpenMPI loaded by default
on the A100 nodes*, which is a version of OpenMPI configured for these
nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this new environment,
we recommend requesting an [interactive node](#software).

For reference, the default environment on the A100 (gpu-q) nodes is provided by loading a module as follows:

```
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
```

However since the CPU type on gpu-q is different from any other on the
cluster, it is not recommended to build software intended to run on gpu-q on a
different flavour of node.

Furthermore, each GPU should be assigned to its closest set of CPU cores (NUMA domain). 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0}}, 'text': 'Rapids HBM[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids-hbm "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

user_input:
Dear HPC team,
Please find attached a purchase order for 2,000 CPU hours of service level 2 , to be credited to Emily Lines.
Best wishes,
Anne Baranger
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SL2 Credit Provisioning / Procurement Workflow
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The requester has sent a PO for SL2 CPU hours to be credited for Emily Lines. From the initial message alone, it is not clear whether these hours should go onto an existing SL2 project or whether a new project account is needed. The correct next step is to confirm the target SL2 account and, if none exists, follow the normal project-creation and approval path.

ACTION
1) Ask whether the credits are for an existing SL2 project or a new one.
2) If no suitable existing SL2 CPU project exists, follow the normal approval process to create one.
3) Add the required users to the target project once the account path is confirmed.
4) If an existing account is intended instead, request the exact target project code.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Anne,

Before we can apply the purchased CPU hours, we need to confirm whether they are for an existing SL2 CPU project or whether a new project needs to be created.

If there is no suitable existing SL2 CPU project for this work, we would need to follow the normal approval process before creating one and applying the hours.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC Service Level Management [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [3]
Portal Action Required: No [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has provided a purchase order for 2,000 CPU hours of service level 2 to be credited to Emily Lines [1]. According to the HPC service level policies, Service Level 2 (SL2) is designed for groups with ad-hoc usage requirements, and credits are created at the time of purchase [5].

ACTION
To add the purchased CPU hours to Emily Lines' account, the support team will use the gdeposit command, specifying the number of hours and the project name [3]. The exact command will be:
```
gdeposit -z 2000 -p <project_name>
```
Replace `<project_name>` with the actual project name associated with Emily Lines.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
We have received your purchase order for 2,000 CPU hours of service level 2. We will add these hours to Emily Lines' account shortly. Please note that these hours will be available for use until exhausted, and any unused hours will not be transferred to the next quarter [5].

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The HPC service level policies dictate that Service Level 2 (SL2) credits are created at the time of purchase and are available for use until exhausted [5].
- The support team must ensure that the correct project name is used when adding the purchased CPU hours to Emily Lines' account [3].

[5] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/policies.html::chunk::0003

A service level is attached to a project, which is a group of users led by a principal investigator (PI) who controls (or who can apply for) a line of funding for HPC.

## Service Levels[¶](#service-levels "Permalink to this headline")

The current Service Levels (SLs), which came into operation in November 2017 and which apply to the Peta4 and Wilkes3 clusters, are described below.

Funding units are in the form of usage credits. What these credits represent depends on the type of cluster. For the CPU cluster (Peta4) 1 credit = 1 CPU core for 1 hour. For the GPU cluster (Wilkes3) 1 credit = 1 GPU for 1 hour.

Accounting periods or quarters are three month periods of the year running 1st February - 30th April, 1st May - 31st July, 1st August - 31st October and 1st November - 31st January.

```

and will be allocated the number of CPUs required for the number of tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory.

By default, the cclake partition provides 1 CPU and 3420MB of RAM per task, and the cclake-himem partition provides 1 CPU and 6840MB per task.

Requesting more CPUs per task, or more memory per task, may both increase the number of CPUs allocated (and hence the charge). It is more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3420MB per task to the cclake-himem partition since more memory per CPU is natively available there.

NB Hyperthreading is disabled so there is no distinction between CPUs and cores.

In addition to *cclake* there are now also *icelake* and *sapphire* partitions which work in a similar way. The precise numbers of CPUs per node and mount of memory available per CPU differ across these types since the hardware differs. Please see the directory

```
/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/
```

for template submission scripts appropriate to each of these node types (including for GPU).

**Wilkes3-GPU**

Wilkes3-GPU assigns usage in units of GPU hours. By convention projects containing GPU hours have names ending in -GPU.

Jobs require the partition ampere, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
```

## Introduction[¶](#introduction "Permalink to this headline")

The Research Computing Service aims to help deliver cutting edge research using innovative technology within the broad area of data-centric HPC. It is responsible for the hosting, system support, scientific support and service delivery of several large supercomputing and research data storage resources for the University of Cambridge research community.

The University-wide service is run as a self-sustaining cost centre and therefore must recover all costs incurred by the capital depreciation and running costs of the computer equipment, plus additional scientific support costs incurred to help increase the useful scientific output achieved from the machine. To this end the computational equipment within the Service is run as a Major Research Facility under the Full Economic Costing (fEC) funding model. Under this model, units of use are priced and research staff should determine how many units they require for a particular project, explicitly include these costs within a grant application for the project, and then pass this funding back to the HPC Service as a direct cost.

user_input:
Hi there,
I manage a Research Data store for the “kstarkstar” project:
Directory: rds-mk652-kstarkstar
Project ID: bUoO2gzww9Q
It is about to expire. I want to extend the license for another year (for all 5TB) with all of the same permissions. I raised a PO have requested a new data store but think I may have made a mistake here. I don’t want a new storage area, I just want to extend the existing one.
Please can you resolve my mistake?
The PO for the extension is attached.
Many thanks,
Matt
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Renewal / Procurement Correction
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants to extend an existing storage project but believes they may have accidentally requested a new storage purchase instead. The safe handling is to stop them from activating anything new until support has verified which service record should be extended and whether the mistaken request can be corrected or needs to be cancelled or reissued.

ACTION
1) Ask the user not to activate the mistaken newly purchased storage project.
2) Confirm which existing project is the one that should be extended.
3) Check internally whether the mistaken request can be removed or converted.
4) If cancellation or reissue is required, direct the user to the appropriate purchasing or finance route.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Matt,

Please leave the accidentally purchased storage project unallocated while we verify whether the mistaken request can be corrected or whether it needs to be cancelled and reissued.

Once we confirm the correct path, we will tell you whether any purchasing or finance action is needed on your side.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Licence Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has a Research Data store for the "kstarkstar" project that is about to expire and wants to extend the license for another year with the same permissions [1]. The user has raised a PO but thinks they may have made a mistake by requesting a new data store instead of extending the existing one [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm with the user that they want to extend the existing Research Data store (rds-mk652-kstarkstar) for another year with the same permissions [1].
2. Advise the user to cancel the existing PO and raise a new one for the extension of the existing store [1].
3. Direct the user to the Self Service Storage Portal to complete the extension process [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm the exact project ID and directory name for the Research Data store [1]?
- Have you already received a quote for the extension, and if so, can you please share it with us [1]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To extend your existing Research Data store (rds-mk652-kstarkstar), please cancel the existing PO and raise a new one for the extension [1]. You can then complete the extension process in the Self Service Storage Portal [2]. If you have any issues or concerns, please get back in touch.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user has the necessary permissions and access to extend the Research Data store [1].
- Verify that the user has cancelled the existing PO before raising a new one to avoid any duplicate charges [1].

Important

The School of Biological Sciences ‘SBS’ resource allocation scheme ended on 31 Dec 2022.

Any PIs and users still wishing to retain their current storage projects should respond to the automated renewal notices sent from the self-service portal prior to 31 Dec. Or alternatively, contact the servicedesk at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance in identifying the relevant projects or migrating the data elsewhere.

## Key Deadlines[¶](#key-deadlines "Permalink to this headline")

### From 1 January 2023[¶](#from-1-january-2023 "Permalink to this headline")

* Any storage projects not yet renewed have been flagged as ‘suspended’ in the
  storage portal.
* Data owners and managers can still generate quotes and submit purchase orders to renew,
  which will be automatically backdated to be effective from 1 Jan.
* Access to data is still possible during the 30 day grace period for late renewals,
  after which access to the data will be lost.

### From 30 January 2023 (end of grace period)[¶](#from-30-january-2023-end-of-grace-period "Permalink to this headline")

* User access to unrenewed projects will be lost.
* All data in projects will **only be accessible on request**.
  Access will be provided in a read-only mode in cases where it is necessary to migrate
  data to an alternative location on CSD3 or offsite.

Warning

The WBIC platform contained a number of different storage areas for
users, all of which will be **retired** with the closure of the platform.

All data should be migrated to an alternative storage platform on
CSD3 or downloaded to another location before the platform is
shut-down.

Below we outline alternative services on CSD3 for each storage area that
are available for purchase so that you can replicate the WBIC
environment as best as possible.

#### /lustre[¶](#lustre "Permalink to this headline")

The WBIC dedicated Lustre filesystem (/lustre) is replaced by our [RDS
storage service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store)
which provides equivalent large-scale scratch storage.

All CSD3 users get a free 1TB personal scratch space, but all
group-shared capacity must be purchased through our [Storage self-service
gateway](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/storage/RDS/)
Owners of group directories will be contacted separately to make arrangements.

#### /home[¶](#home "Permalink to this headline")

All WBIC users had 100GB of /home space, however on CSD3 this is reduced
to 40GB. Users with usage greater than 40GB will be required to either
reduce their usage under this amount, or to purchase additional RFS or
RDS storage and move excess data to these areas.

#### /data[¶](#data "Permalink to this headline")

WBIC users also had access to the /data storage location, which benefited
from the same resilient storage platform as /home, including hourly
snapshots and data replication, designed for datasets requiring durable
backups.

migrate the credentials used to authenticate you to the PACS server,
from your WBIC home-directory to CSD3.

This can be performed as follows:

```
user@wbic-gate-3:~$ dcm_migrate
```

## Extending Access to CSD3[¶](#extending-access-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

It is the user’s responsibilty to ensure they have access to the service for the required time needed for their activity.
Users who are part of the University of Cambridge, who seek to extend their CSD3 account access past their initial university credentials expiry, will need to extend their student or visitor status with their university department. Doing so will extend their access to their university credentials and in turn, prolong access to the CSD3 service.
If a student is unable to extend their student status, they will need to register as a vistor with their department to maintain access to their university credentials for use with the CSD3 service.

For more on the University’s leaving procedure, please see this page: [help.uis.cam.ac.uk/service/accounts-passwords/leaving](https://help.uis.cam.ac.uk/service/accounts-passwords/leaving/)
